flashcards.front: What is the core purpose of the Sensing & Actuation Layer in an IoT smart home system?
flashcards.back: To interact directly with the physical environment. Sensors (e.g., temperature, motion) collect data, while actuators (e.g., smart switches, motorized locks) perform physical actions based on commands.

flashcards.front: Name two common wireless protocols used in the Connectivity Layer of home automation and one key characteristic of each.
flashcards.back: 1) Wi-Fi: High bandwidth, common in homes, but higher power consumption. 2) Zigbee/Z-Wave: Low-power, mesh networking, designed for battery-operated sensors and low-data-rate devices.

flashcards.front: What are the primary roles of the Processing Hub (Gateway) in a typical smart home architecture?
flashcards.back: 1) Data Aggregation: Collects data from all connected sensors. 2) Protocol Translation: Bridges different communication protocols. 3) Local Logic Execution: Runs automation rules without cloud dependency. 4) Secure Gateway: Manages secure connection to the cloud.

flashcards.front: How does a case study illustrate the importance of a systems approach in IoT home automation design?
flashcards.back: It shows that all layers (Sensing, Connectivity, Processing, UI) must be designed to work together seamlessly. A failure in one layer (e.g., poor connectivity) breaks the entire user experience, highlighting interdependency.

flashcards.front: From a case study perspective, what is a major challenge when integrating devices from different manufacturers into a single home automation system?
flashcards.back: Lack of standardization and interoperability. Devices may use different protocols, require separate hubs, or use proprietary ecosystems, leading to a fragmented user experience. Solutions include open standards (like Matter) or hub-based integration.

flashcards.front: What is the 3-tier architecture of a smart home system?
flashcards.back: 1. Perception Layer: Sensors and actuators. 2. Network Layer: Gateway/hub device. 3. Application Layer: Cloud and user applications.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between Zigbee and WiFi for smart home IoT?
flashcards.back: Zigbee is low-power with mesh networking, ideal for battery sensors. WiFi is high-bandwidth but power-hungry, suited for cameras and streaming devices.

flashcards.front: Why is LoRaWAN preferred over WiFi for agricultural IoT?
flashcards.back: LoRaWAN offers long-range communication (several km) with very low power consumption, essential for battery-powered sensors spread across large fields where WiFi range is insufficient.

flashcards.front: What are the three main IoT levels demonstrated by smart home and agriculture case studies?
flashcards.back: Level 3 (Smart Home): Single node with cloud analysis. Level 4 (Agriculture): Multiple nodes with both local and cloud analysis. Both demonstrate M2M communication.

flashcards.front: What is the logical design flow of a precision agriculture system?
flashcards.back: Sensor -> Cloud -> Analytics -> Actuator. Sensors collect soil data, cloud processes it, analytics determine irrigation needs, and actuators (valves) execute irrigation.
